text:
journal homepage: www.journals.elsevier.com/world-neurosurgery-x
The global state of cranioplasty practice following cranial decompression for traumatic brain injury: a provider survey ☆
Sara Venturini a,* , Saniya Mediratta a , Tobias J. Adams a , Harry Mee a , Bhagavatula Indira Devi b , Tariq Khan c , Andres M. Rubiano d,e , Franco Servadei f , Adel Helmy g , Angelos Kolias a , Peter J. Hutchinson a

a Global Health Research Group on Acquired Brain and Spine Injury, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom
b Department of Neurosurgery, National Institute of Mental Health and Neuro Sciences, NIMHANS, Bengaluru, Karnataka, India
c Department of Neurosurgery, North Western General and Research Hospital, Peshawar, Pakistan
d Neuroscience Institute, Universidad El Bosque, Bogota, Colombia
e INUB Meditech Group, Meditech Foundation, Cali, Colombia

e INUB Meditech Group, Meditech Foundation, Cali, Colombia
f Global Neurosurgery Programme, Besta Neurological Institute, Milan, Italy
g Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Clinical Neurosciences, University of Cambridge, Cambridge, United Kingdom
Keywords: Traumatic brain injury Decompressive craniectomy Cranioplasty Global neurosurgery

Traumatic  brain  injury  (TBI)  can  cause  intracranial  hypertension through a variety of pathomechanisms. 1 To treat this, decompressive craniectomy  (DC)  is  a  common  and  effective  operation  involving removing  a  portion  of  the  skull  and  opening  the  underlying  dura mater. 2 -4 For patients who survive, cranial reconstruction (cranioplasty)

Objective: For patients undergoing decompressive craniectomy for TBI, cranioplasty facilitates rehabilitation and reintegration  into society. Cranioplasty practice is  poorly documented globally. Challenges including lack of materials and technical skills disproportionately affect LMICs. This study evaluates global cranioplasty practice and the extent to which barriers preclude patients from accessing cranial reconstruction.

Methods: An international survey was disseminated to centres performing cranioplasty for TBI. Survey questions addressed baseline hospital information, cranioplasty indications, barriers and techniques, follow-up. Centres in HICs and LMICs were compared.

Results: 101 responses were received (86 individual institutions, 39 countries). Variation in practice was seen globally, and between HICs and LMICs. Autologous bone was the most common material used. Titanium and polymethylmethacrylate were the most used artificial implants. LMIC sites were more likely to store bone flaps in the patient ' s  abdomen, while HICs had more access to 3D printing. Lack of infection and good neurological recovery were the commonest eligibility criteria. Cost of

neurological recovery were the commonest eligibility criteria. Cost of materials/operation and unavailable materials were the commonest barriers. Responders suggested easier access to cheaper materials would significantly improve access. Cranioplasty associated costs were higher than the country ' s GNI per capita in 7 cases. Less than 50 % of patients without cranioplasty had access to protective equipment. Less than a quarter of respondents stated patients had access to brain injury
than a quarter of respondents stated patients had access to brain injury charities, and over 50 % believed stigma affects their patients.

Conclusions: Variation in cranioplasty practice was confirmed. Barriers limiting access were identified, specifically,  availability  of  materials  and  operation-related  costs.  These  findings  can  inform  context-specific  interventions to overcome current challenges.

is usually indicated to repair the defect. In addition to restoring cerebral protection and improving cosmesis, cranioplasty can improve cerebral blood flow, neurological function, 5,6 and may aid physical and cognitive rehabilitation. 7 Possible complications include infection, hydrocephalus, intracranial haemorrhage and seizures. 8,9
Cranioplasty is also performed following DC for indications other than TBI; primarily following ischaemic stroke but sometimes following

☆ Previous presentations: results of the study were presented at the Society of British Neurological Surgeons (SBNS) meetings.
* Corresponding author. Division of Neurosurgery, Department of Clinical Neurosciences University of Cambridge, Cambridge, CB2 0QQ, United Kingdom. E-mail address: sv465@cam.ac.uk (S. Venturini).
spontaneous  intracranial  haemorrhage.  This  study  addresses  cranioplasty performed following TBI.

There is variation in cranioplasty practice and the optimal material for reconstruction has yet to be agreed upon. 10,11 Synthetic materials can be used including the thermoplastics polymethylmethacrylate and polyetheretherketone; titanium in the form of plates, malleable meshes and 3D porous implants; and osteoconductive, porous hydroxyapatite. A common  alternative  is  autologous  bone,  which  can  be  stored  in  an abdominal  pocket  or  undergo  extracorporeal  cryopreservation, 12 but
abdominal  pocket  or  undergo  extracorporeal  cryopreservation, 12 but has the potential issue of bone flap resorption. 13

The timing from bone removal to cranioplasty is variable, largely owing to inconsistent definitions of ' early cranioplasty ' and mixed results from investigating associations between timing, complications 10,14,15 and neurological outcome.

To address variation in clinical practice within and between countries, the first consensus-based recommendations for cranioplasty were published in 2021. 10 The ability to offer cranioplasty is influenced by the available  technical  skills,  neurosurgical  facilities,  and  presence  of institutional frameworks for treatment and follow-up. Challenges within these  factors  are  likely  greater  in  low-  and  middle-income  countries (LMICs),  yet  the  extent  to  which  barriers  preclude

countries (LMICs),  yet  the  extent  to  which  barriers  preclude  patients  from accessing cranioplasty has not been formally investigated. Therefore, this study aims to evaluate global practice around cranioplasty for TBI, and identify barriers to access, as perceived by practicing professionals.
A cross-sectional study was performed through dissemination of an online survey (Supplementary Material 1). A summary of the domains addressed in the survey is presented in Table 1.

The  survey  was  designed  based  on  recent  cranioplasty  literature, including the recent consensus statement, 10 to capture key aspects of care. It was created using Google Forms (Google) as this is free-to-access and accessible globally via smartphone or computer. The survey was piloted by the NIHR Global Health Research Group on Acquired Brain and Spine Injury to ensure questions were clear and relevant to clinicians working across a range of settings. Feedback was sought on survey
working across a range of settings. Feedback was sought on survey content, structure, ease of completion.

A systematic a priori dissemination strategy was developed to capture responses across World Bank Income Groups and WHO Regions and was phased to enable response rate monitoring.
Phase 1 consisted of invitation directly via email. Respondents were selected from institutions across WHO Regions, aiming to obtain one response per neurosurgery centre. Invitation and participation logs were kept throughout. Members of the following groups were initially invited to participate.

NIHR Global Health Research Group on Acquired Brain and Spine Injury
Participating  centres  of  the  Global  Neurotrauma  Outcomes  Study (GNOS) run by the NIHR GHRGABSI
The second phase expanded invitation through neurosurgical societies, including the WFNS newsletter. Phase three utilised dissemination via social media platforms.

Data was analysed using Microsoft Excel and R. 16,17 A descriptive representation of the data was used. Gross national income (GNI) was used  to  group  countries  into  high,  upper-middle,  lower-middle,  and low-income groups. Comparison between country income groups was performed using Fisher ' s Exact tests. Upper middle-, lower middle- and low-income countries were grouped into the LMIC group for statistical comparison  due  to  low  numbers  in  each  category,  when  comparing groups

due  to  low  numbers  in  each  category,  when  comparing groups  according  to  the  study  variables.  Statistics  comparisons  were then  performed  between  the  high-income  country  (HIC)  and  LMIC groups. A p -value of < 0.05 indicated statistical significance.

The World Bank was used as the source for GNI and GDP (gross domestic product) data at country level, for 2021, the most recent year available. 18 Where  multiple  responses  were  provided  for  the  same institution, discrepancies were identified and respondents individually contacted by email for clarification.

101 responses were received. After removing duplicates ( n = 13) and responses  from  students  ( n = 2),  responses  from  86  independent healthcare centres across 39 countries, spanning 5 continents, were used in  subsequent  analysis  (except  free-text  questions  seeking  individual opinion, where all responses were included). The global distribution of participating centres is summarised in Fig. 1. Countries with more than two  centres  represented  were  the  United  Kingdom  (12.8  %

more than two  centres  represented  were  the  United  Kingdom  (12.8  %  of  institutions surveyed), Italy (8.1 %), Ethiopia and India (7.0 % each) and Brazil (5.8 %), Kenya (5.7 %), Germany (4.7 %), United States (4.7 %) and Malaysia (3.5 %). 71 institutions (82.6 %) were tertiary centres and 84 (95.5 %) were in urban settings.

Baseline information from participating institutions is available in Table 2. Participating centres were similar with regards to respondent profession,  hospital  type,  setting  and  type  of  funding  across  income groups (Fisher ' s exact, all p > 0.05), but hospitals in lower middle- or low income-countries were statistically more likely to charge elective care or all care (elective and emergency) to patients compared to hospitals  in  high  or  upper  middle-income  countries  (Fisher ' s
to hospitals  in  high  or  upper  middle-income  countries  (Fisher ' s  exact, p < 0.001).

Most centres (55, 64 %) offered cranioplasty to all eligible patients following DC and the commonest eligibility criteria were lack of infection (66 centres, 77 % of total), good neurological recovery (53, 62 %), and DC bone flap size (31, 36 %). There was no significant difference between HIC and LMIC groups (Fisher ' s exact, p > 0.05).

Sixty-one  centres  (71  %)  estimated  performing  between  1  and  5 cranioplasty procedures each month with more than half of all centres (44)  estimating  over  75  %  of  patients  undergoing  DC  proceeded  to cranioplasty, with HIC and LMIC groups not differing significantly ( p > 0.05). All HIC sites reported performing between 1 and 10 cranioplasties per  month,  but  LMIC  centres  exhibited  more  variation  with  4  often performing  none  and  4  high-volume  centres  performing
with  4  often performing  none  and  4  high-volume  centres  performing  over  10  a month.

Fig. 1. Map displaying the geographical distribution of respondents. Different colours on the gradient represent greater number of responses per country . Responses were received from the following countries: Austria, Bangladesh, Bolivia, Brazil, Burundi, Cameroon, Colombia, Ecuador, Estonia, Ethiopia, Finland, Germany, Greece, India, Indonesia, Ireland, Italy, Kenya, Madagascar, Malaysia, Mexico, Mozambique, Myanmar, Nepal, Nigeria, Poland, Rwanda, Singapore, South Africa, Spain, Sri Lanka,
Nepal, Nigeria, Poland, Rwanda, Singapore, South Africa, Spain, Sri Lanka, Sudan, Syria, Tanzania, The Gambia, United Arab Emirates, United Kingdom, United States, Vietnam.

Table 2 Baseline information of the participating institutions, grouped by country income groups.
The most common timeframe from DC to cranioplasty (35 centres, 41 %) was between 6 weeks and 3 months; followed by between 3- and 6-months after DC (32 centres, 37 %). Nine centres indicated a timeframe of up to 6 weeks post -DC and a further 9 described cranioplasty usually occurring 6 months or more after DC. HIC and LMIC sites showed comparable results ( p = 0.668).

The commonest cranioplasty material used was autologous bone (53 centres, 62 %) and 11 hospitals (13 %), 10 of which were located in
LMICs, only implanted autologous bone. Titanium (47 centres, 55 %) and polymethylmethacrylate (45 centres, 52 %) were the commonest artificial implants. Other implants included polyether-ether-ketone (29 centres, 34 %) and hydroxyapatite (21 centres, 24 %).

Regarding artificial  implants,  52  hospitals  (61  %)  used  computer planning techniques including computer aided design/computer aided manufacturer (CAD/CAM) to create custom implants for at least some cranioplasties. Of these, 40 (77 %) always outsourced this design process whilst 13 centres (25 %) used in-house CAD/CAM design for some implants, 8 (15 %) doing so exclusively, with HICs and LMICs showing a similar  distribution  ( p = 0.384).  Twenty-seven  centres  never  used computer

distribution  ( p = 0.384).  Twenty-seven  centres  never  used computer planning techniques, with a significant majority being located in 24 being in LMICs ( p < 0.001). Forty-five (87 %) institutions of those

using implants utilised 3D-printed implants, with most institutions (35) outsourcing 3D-printed implant production and 9 centres (5/9 in HICs) printing implants in their own facility. Two centres located in LMICs used 3D-printed cranioplasty plates supplied via donation.

Intraoperatively, surgical drain insertion was standard practice in 63 centres (73 %) under passive (23 centres, 27 %) or active drainage (40 centres,  47  %),  with  similar  practice  in  HIC  and  LMIC  groups  ( p = 0.382). Post-cranioplasty antibiotic administration was routine practice in 69 centres (80 %) overall; LMIC sites had significantly higher rates of prophylactic antibiotic use compared to HICs (91 % vs 70 %, p = 0.02).

There was variation regarding the fate of bone flap removed during DC, with a near-three-way split between freezing the flap in hospital or private facilities (28 centres); storing the flap in the patient ' s abdomen (28 centres), and discarding the flap (26 centres) when considering all centres together. When comparing HIC and LMIC sites, LMIC sites were significantly more likely to store the bone flap in the patient ' s abdomen than HIC sites (25 vs 3 sites), and less likely to discard it (22
abdomen than HIC sites (25 vs 3 sites), and less likely to discard it (22 vs 44) or store it frozen ( p < 0.001).

Overall, the commonest barriers to offering cranioplasty were 1) lack of  patient  follow-up;  2)  cost  of  plate  materials;  3)  patient  difficulty accessing hospital facilities; 4) operation and hospital stay costs; 5) lack of available plate materials; 6) and patient objection due to religious or ethical principles. LMIC sites reported that operation costs, cost of the implant,  lack  of  cranioplasty  plates,  and  difficulty  accessing  hospital facilities were more frequent barriers

difficulty  accessing  hospital facilities were more frequent barriers than HIC sites ( p < 0.05 for all comparisons). Both groups reported lack of follow up and patient objections equally ( p > 0.05). Additional free text suggestions included: an ' overburdened system ' with limited availability of operating theatres, patient beds and staff; other surgeries taking precedence resulting in large waiting  lists;  and  patients ' relatives  opposing  cranioplasty, favouring non-surgical
and  patients ' relatives  opposing  cranioplasty, favouring non-surgical management.

Forty-three respondents (50 %) suggested interventions to improve access to cranioplasty. Thirteen suggested reducing costs of materials or care. Ten (9 from LMICs), suggested increasing availability of operating theatres,  patient  beds,  and  staff.  Additional  suggestions  included improving  access  to  cranioplasty  equipment,  3D  printing  and  clinic services (8 responses, 19 %) and improving availability of materials (7 responses, 16 %). Five responses (12 %) suggested better
of materials (7 responses, 16 %). Five responses (12 %) suggested better educating the public about cranioplasty.

Respondents were asked to estimate the costs attributed to cranioplasty. The median (IQR [range]) cost per cranioplasty implant, estimated by 67 institutions (78 %), was 1000 USD (375 -4850 [7 -20000]), with patients required to cover part of the cost in 14 centres (16 %) and the full cost in 26 centres (30 %). Median cost per procedure, estimated by 72 institutions, was 2000 USD (525 -6000 [15 -20000]) with patients covering it in full in 25 centres (29 %) and part of it in 15 centres (17 %).

Procedural costs were compared to each country ' s GNI per capita using  World  Bank  data.  Estimated  procedure-associated  costs  were greater than the country ' s GNI per capita in 7 institutions (8 %) across 6 different LMICs; and more than 50 % of GNI per capita in 13 institutions (15 %) across 11 countries - all but one located in an LMIC. Focussing on cranioplasty materials (excluding procedure-related expenditure), cost was greater than GNI per capita in 3 countries, all LMICs. Centres

cost was greater than GNI per capita in 3 countries, all LMICs. Centres in Sudan ( n = 1) and Kenya ( n = 2) reported the estimated cost of cranioplasty operations (both greater than the country ' s respective GNI) but did not report material costs alone. Fig. 2 summarises the relationship between GNI and cranioplasty operation (a) and material (b) costs across participating centres.
Estimated procedure costs were also greater than GDP per capita in the same institutions; and more than 50 % of GDP per capita in 14 institutions (16 %), all but one in LMICs.

Eighty-one institutions (95 %) followed-up patients postcranioplasty, with similar rates in LMICs and HICs (100 % vs 92 %, p = 0.075). LMIC sites were more likely to follow patients up within 4 weeks of surgery (71 % vs 36 % in HICs, p = 0.005). Follow up in HIC centres was equally split into thirds between the three time windows: within 4 weeks, 4 -8 and 8 -12 weeks after surgery. In-person appointments were the commonest follow-up modality in all groups (80 sites, 93 %).

For  in-patient  settings,  physiotherapy  was  the  most  commonly available service (66 centres, 77 %) followed by speech and language (50 centres, 58 %) and occupational therapy (41 centres, 47 %). Distributions were comparable between HIC and LMIC sites ( p = 0.821). In community  settings  following  patient  discharge,  availability  of  all rehabilitation services declined except for occupational therapy (increased  to  44  centres,  51  %)  and  private  rehabilitation  services
to  44  centres,  51  %)  and  private  rehabilitation  services (increased from 9 [11 %] to 17 [20 %] centres).

In 37 centres, DC patients not undergoing cranioplasty lacked access to  protective  equipment  such  as  helmets.  Thirty  of  these  sites  were located in LMICs, and LMIC sites had significantly less access to protective equipment compared to HIC sites (30 [77 %] vs 12 [32 %], p < 0.001).  Twenty-one  centres  overall  stated  cranioplasty  patients  had access to charities or support groups for brain injury, with significantly more access in HICs (15 (54 %) compared to LMICs (6 [13 %]), p <
more access in HICs (15 (54 %) compared to LMICs (6 [13 %]), p < 0.001.

Forty-five respondents (52 %) believed stigma affects their patients following DC. Thirty-nine free-text responses described stigma faced by patients. Twenty specified alterations in physical appearance as a source of stigma, whilst 6 attributed it to disability resulting from the medical episode.  Two  consultant  neurosurgeons  from  separate  institutions  in Ethiopia described how local beliefs may negatively impact patients:

' The  community  may  interpret  the  defect  as  something  which  came following evil doing ' , and
' People in the countryside may think it ' s not as a result of trauma rather a spiritual curse that there is a craniectomy defect ' .
One neurosurgeon in South Africa highlighted that in younger individuals,  head  trauma  is  often  stigmatised  for  its  association  with ' criminal activity ' :
' In younger people, head trauma is often associated with criminal activity, gangsterism ' .

Societal  stigma  from  the  inability  to  return  to  everyday  life  and employment was highlighted in multiple responses:
' Obvious defect, cosmetically and aesthetically unpleasant until cranioplasty done. Patient cannot freely participate in normal activity ' ,
' People avoid them because of the missing bone and generally become unemployable as people feel they are a liability ' ,
' Many times, the patient who has TBI who is recovered and able to work, is rejected for work due to employer fears ' .

Fig. 2. Scatter plots showing the ratio of cranioplasty (CP) associated costs reported by each centre relative to the country ' s GNI per capita, grouped by income groups (HIC, UMIC, LMIC, LIC). Each centre is represented by a dot on the chart, income groups are represented by the four colours. Plot A shows the cranioplasty operation/procedural costs, plot B shows the cranioplasty material costs. The vertical lines indicate when the ratio of cranioplasty-related costs to GNI reaches 50 % (ratio

when the ratio of cranioplasty-related costs to GNI reaches 50 % (ratio = 0.5, dotted line) and 100 % (ratio = 1, solid line). Centres where the ratio of CP/GNI is greater than 1, meaning the cost of CP is greater than the country ' s GNI, are labelled in both graphs.

This study aimed to evaluate current global practices around cranioplasty following DC for TBI and identify barriers to performing cranioplasty. The 50-question survey received responses from 86 healthcare  centres  across  39  countries.  Our  results,  representing  one fifth  of  countries  worldwide,  are  the  widest  representation  of  global cranioplasty practice to date.

One key message from the Lancet Commission on TBI is that people in  LMICs  are  disproportionately  affected  by  TBI,  with  most  injuries caused by road traffic incidents. Substantial  disparities  in  care  exist, with little infrastructure available for emergency pre-hospital care and little access to post-acute care. 19 Twenty-six countries included in our study  (67  %)  are  classified  as  LMICs. 20 Concerning  participant  demographics, information from 78 centres (87 %) was provided

participant  demographics, information from 78 centres (87 %) was provided by fully qualified  neurosurgeons  or  neurosurgery  residents.  All  other  centres were represented by qualified medical doctors. Therefore, it is likely each survey response is a good representation of its institution ' s cranioplasty practice.

It is not possible to deduce from the data how representative each surveyed centre is of national practice. This would require more data per nation  and  detailed  understanding  of  the  degree  of  practice  standardisation across each nation. Most institutions surveyed were tertiary centres located in urban settings, in line with the specialised nature of cranioplasty practice. Cranioplasty can be classified as a tertiary prevention intervention, facilitating recovery, and is expected to be
prevention intervention, facilitating recovery, and is expected to be almost exclusively performed in specialised centres, unlike DC.

Most centres offered cranioplasty to all eligible patients following DC. Lack of infection and good neurological recovery were the commonest indications appearing consistently across LMICs and HICs. Size of the DC flap, on the other hand, was an indication selected predominantly by LMIC centres (31 centres total, 23 located in LMICs) possibly owing to availability of resources limiting maximum cranioplasty size. Most institutions estimated monthly cranioplasty procedure burden at

between 1 and 5 and estimated over 75 % of patients undergoing DC proceeded to cranioplasty. Interestingly, across LMIC sites there were four high-volume centres performing over 10 cranioplasties a month on average. This could be explained by most participating sites being urban referral centres, and neurosurgery care being centralised in many LMICs in  few  high-volume  institutions.  The  lower  numbers  of  monthly  cranioplasties compared to DC could be accounted for by patient mortality
cranioplasties compared to DC could be accounted for by patient mortality from their initial injuries.

Lack of patient follow-up was the highest rated barrier to offering cranioplasty,  followed  by  cost  of  plate  materials;  difficulty  accessing hospital facilities; operation and hospital costs; lack of available plate materials; and patient objection due to religious or ethical principles. Common barriers equally reported by HIC and LMIC sites were identified  (lack  of  follow  up  and  patient  objection  to  surgery),  but  costs, availability of implants and access to hospital were

but  costs, availability of implants and access to hospital were more common in LMICs, possibly owing to less resilient health systems. Of the 20 additional barriers suggested by respondents via free text, representatives of 9 centres (across 7 LMICs, 2 HICs) referred to an ' overburdened system ' in which availability of operating theatres, patient beds and staff limited access,  with  other  surgeries  taking  precedence.  Representatives  of  4 centres reported patients ' relatives

precedence.  Representatives  of  4 centres reported patients ' relatives favouring non-surgical management instead of cranioplasty. This highlights areas that could be targeted with future interventions.

Forty-three respondents suggested interventions to improve access to cranioplasty. Key suggestions focussed on reducing costs of materials used  or  care  involved.  Nine  LMIC  respondents  suggested  increasing availability of operating theatres, beds, and staff. This is in keeping with previous evidence on barriers to surgical care. It is aligned to the findings of the Lancet Commission on Global Surgery, identifying a large gap and inequity in distribution of the surgical workforce in

a large gap and inequity in distribution of the surgical workforce in LMICs, setting a target of 100 % of countries with at least 20 surgical, anaesthetic, and obstetric physicians per 100 000 population by 2030. 21

Improving patient and public education about cranioplasty was also suggested,  indicating  that  both  patient  and  carers ' education  are important avenues for global policy change to target improved access to cranioplasty.

The most common timeframes from DC to cranioplasty were between 6 weeks and 3 months and between 3- and 6-months. This reflects how recommendations for cranioplasty timing are variable, owing to inconsistent definitions of ' early cranioplasty ' and mixed results from studies investigating the relationship between timing, complications 10,14,15 and neurological outcome. A recent meta-analysis found no significant difference in complications in relation to timing except that cranioplasty within

in complications in relation to timing except that cranioplasty within  90  days  of  DC  was  associated  with  increased  odds  of  hydrocephalus. 9 Despite this, the most common practice for our centres surveyed was cranioplasty within 90 days.

There was considerable variation in first-line practice regarding bone removed  during  DC,  with  a  near-three-way  overall  split  between extracorporeal cryopreservation in hospital or private facilities, abdominal  pocket  storage,  and  disposal.  This  reflects  the  available literature, showing that abdominal pocket storage and extracorporeal cryopreservation display similar  outcomes. 12 Nearly all hospitals (25/28) where abdominal pocket storage was first line were in LMICs, likely

where abdominal pocket storage was first line were in LMICs, likely  owing  to  its  lower  cost  (despite  requiring  additional  surgery) relative to cryopreservation or use of artificial implants. This contrasts with HIC hospitals where there was an equal split between cryopreservation (48 %) or discarding the bone flap (48 %) but few sites stored the bone flap in the patient ' s abdomen (3).

The material used by most institutions for cranioplasty was autologous bone. Twelve hospitals (11 in LMICs) only implanted autologous bone, likely owing to its biocompatibility and low cost. Despite their high cost, 22 titanium and polymethylmethacrylate were the most used artificial implants. The distribution reflects that the optimal material for reconstruction has yet to be agreed upon. 10 A meta-analysis by Henry et al demonstrated  the  highest  revision rate with titanium, whilst

et al demonstrated  the  highest  revision rate with titanium, whilst polyether-ether-ketone was associated with the lowest risk of re-operation. 11 A subsequent meta-analysis showed risk of infection was 1.62 times higher when polymethylmethacrylate was used compared with autologous bone and that length of stay after titanium cranioplasty was on average 3.62 days shorter compared with autologous bone. 23 Authors concluded ' there is not strong enough evidence available to make comprehensive
' there is not strong enough evidence available to make comprehensive conclusions regarding the risk profiles of various cranioplasty materials across multiple outcomes ' . 23

Regarding CAD/CAM design of implants, significant differences in access  were  seen  between  HICs,  where  support  was  often  used,  and LMICs where this was available less than 50 % of the time. It is highly likely the number of centres in LMICs utilising 3D-printing technology will drastically increase in the future due to the emergence of personal desktop  3D-printers  and  open-source  software  lowering  costs. 22,24,25 However, the most cost-effective  cranioplasty  implant  remains
22,24,25 However, the most cost-effective  cranioplasty  implant  remains  to  be determined with mixed results from studies to date. 26,27

When investigating costs and economic impact associated with cranioplasty procedures, we observed that needing a cranioplasty operation can  result  in  impoverishing  or  catastrophic  expenditure  for  patients. Respondents were asked to estimate the costs attributed to cranioplasty in  their  centres.  Of  the  67  institutions  providing  data  on  the  cost  of cranioplasty implants, 14 centres stated that patients cover at least part of the cost, and 26 that patients cover the full cost.

cover at least part of the cost, and 26 that patients cover the full cost. Similar results were seen for the cost of a cranioplasty operation. These estimated costs were greater than the country ' s GNI per capita in 7 institutions (all LMICs); and greater than 50 % of GNI per capita in 13 institutions, all but one in a LMIC. Despite the small sample size, this data from several countries suggests the cost of cranioplasty can be incredibly high making it unaffordable, especially where patients

can be incredibly high making it unaffordable, especially where patients and families are responsible for funding elective surgical care. In a few settings, cranioplasty costs were more than double the country ' s GNI. The variation seen may be influenced by income levels within the region and differences in financing methods for healthcare. Patients had to pay out-of-pocket to cover entire procedural costs in 4 of 7 centres and part of the procedural costs in 2 of 7 centres. This is

4 of 7 centres and part of the procedural costs in 2 of 7 centres. This is particularly important considering the Lancet Commission on Global Surgery highlighted protection against impoverishing expenditure  (out-of-pocket  payments  pushing  one  into  poverty  or further into poverty) and catastrophic expenditure (out-of-pocket payments greater than 40 % of household income net of subsistence needs) as core indicators for monitoring universal access to affordable surgical care, setting

for monitoring universal access to affordable surgical care, setting targets of 100 % protection against both by 2023. 28 Whilst assessing whether such targets have been met is beyond the scope of this article,  it  shows  there  is  a  high  likelihood  of  impoverishing  or  catastrophic  expenditure  for  patients,  especially  as  non-medical  costs  of accessing care (e.g. transportation) are not included here. However, it is important to highlight that costs were estimated by respondents
it is important to highlight that costs were estimated by respondents and that future  research  with  objective  measurement  of  costs  is  warranted  to support our findings.

It is important to remember cranioplasty is not the end of a TBI patient ' s care journey. Most institutions (95 %) followed-up patients postcranioplasty, the majority doing so within 3 months of discharge. 93 % followed-up patients via in-person appointment, a portion which could have since reduced due increased virtual physician -patient interactions during  the  COVID-19  pandemic.  Longer  term  follow-up  beyond  3 months is relevant as cranioplasty complications (e.g. implant

beyond  3 months is relevant as cranioplasty complications (e.g. implant displacement, fracture) can occur after a longer interval. It would be important  to  develop  strategies  to  improve  longer-term  follow-up  in resource limited settings, for example utilising smartphone technologies,  overcoming  barriers  such  as  distance  from  hospital.  TBI  affects multiple domains of functioning, and outcomes are affected by personal characteristics and life-course events, consistent with a

by personal characteristics and life-course events, consistent with a multifactorial bio-psycho-socio-ecological  model  of  TBI. 19 Access  to  rehabilitation services following TBI is inconsistent and no evidence-based protocols for  treating  long-term  problems  (including  fatigue,  cognitive  and behavioural  changes)  currently  exist. 19 There  are  reports  of  unmet rehabilitation needs and long-term service use below the prevalence of morbidity  -  with  insufficient  care  in  both
below the prevalence of morbidity  -  with  insufficient  care  in  both  HICs  and  LMICs. 19,29 -31

Furthermore, research suggests rehabilitation referral following TBI is not only influenced by clinical factors but also demographic, social, and organisational  factors. 19 Concerning  rehabilitation  services  offered post-cranioplasty, our data show physiotherapy was offered the most, followed  by  speech  and  language  therapy  and  occupational  therapy. This  is  a  similar  trend  to  that  seen  for  individuals  enrolled  in  the CENTER-TBI study. 29

Less than a quarter of surveyed sites overall stated cranioplasty patients had access to charities or support groups for brain injury. This number was 54 % in HIC and 13 % in LMIC sites. This area could be targeted to improve access to support networks for patients. In addition, over 50 percent of respondents said that they think stigma affects their patients following DC, with commonly reported sources of stigma being alterations  in  physical  appearance  and  disability  resulting  from  the

in  physical  appearance  and  disability  resulting  from  the medical episode. Respondents highlighted the role of local culture and beliefs, suggesting rural communities may perceive patients with craniectomy defects as victims of a ' spiritual curse ' or as something acquired through ' evil doing ' . Social contexts also play a role, for example by associating head trauma with ' criminal activity ' in some settings. Awareness of cultural and societal values in different contexts is
Awareness of cultural and societal values in different contexts is essential to target advocacy and education campaigns to improve awareness at community level.

While centres from several countries have been sampled; the dataset is  not  wholly  representative  of  global  practice.  The  survey  also  introduces  bias  as  responses  were  obtained  from  a  self-selected  cohort where  respondent  volunteered  to  complete  the  survey.  Furthermore, respondents provided estimates based on their local knowledge. Such estimates may have been skewed due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic  on  services  during  the  period  when  the  survey  was

pandemic  on  services  during  the  period  when  the  survey  was  open. Also, whilst efforts were made to limit the number of survey questions to 50, it is possible that decision fatigue could have impacted responses and estimates, particularly in later questions.

Further cross-sectional research accurately quantifying cranioplasty practice is warranted. Current cranioplasty databases are lacking, even in HICs, with the United Kingdom only recently establishing one and reporting findings. 32,33 To effectively impact clinical care and outcomes, interactions are also needed with research funders, regulators, policy makers,  and  patient  organisations. 19 Given  the  barriers  identified, alternative  surgical  approaches  not  requiring  cranioplasty

alternative  surgical  approaches  not  requiring  cranioplasty  should  be investigated.  For  example,  hinge  craniotomy,  where  cranial  decompression is achieved by loosely replacing the bone flap at the end of the surgical procedure, has been utilised in a variety of settings. 34 Systematic evaluation is needed to identify scenarios where hinge craniotomy offers the same clinical benefits, with fewer side effects compared to DC and cranioplasty. In resource-limited settings this may be

compared to DC and cranioplasty. In resource-limited settings this may be particularly beneficial in the longer-term by avoiding complications of craniectomy and cranioplasty which can result in additional unaffordable costs.

When DC is performed, exploring the role of alternatives to cranioplasty including protective helmets should also be investigated formally, as these may be more accessible and affordable than further operations. Our  responses  showed  that  access  to  protective  equipment  is  still limited,  inviting  work  to  assess  innovative  ways  of  improving  this. Similarly,  qualitative  research  should  focus  on  how  different  interventions  can  be  applied  across  cultural  and  societal

interventions  can  be  applied  across  cultural  and  societal  contexts, considering how factors such as stigma and beliefs around outcome can influence the role of interventions such as cranioplasty.

This survey-based study aimed to increase the currently limited evidence  around cranioplasty following  TBI.  Through  surveying  practitioners across a HICs and LMICs, this survey described the current state of  cranioplasty  practice  globally,  highlighting  variations  in  clinical practice and identifying key barriers preventing patients from accessing this service. This can  drive future  work  to  target interventions addressing these barriers, building surgical capacity and equitable
addressing these barriers, building surgical capacity and equitable access to surgery following TBI.
